summary: The paper supports a bounded workflow mechanism for improved coding performance, but the evidence does not isolate which components drive the gain and leaves strong limits on generalization.

bounded_claims.claim_text: The multi-agent workflow succeeded on 4 of 5 coding tasks without human correction, compared with 2 of 5 for the single-agent baseline.
bounded_claims.bounds: This is a reported outcome comparison from a five-task evaluation, not a causal proof; the sample is small and supports only a bounded claim that the workflow outperformed the baseline in this limited test.

bounded_claims.claim_text: In a roughly 180K-line Next.js repository, the system reportedly implemented complex features and bug fixes within a single generation cycle.
bounded_claims.bounds: This supports feasibility in one reported repository setting, not broad performance across repositories; the accompanying case detail also shows at least one task required a failed test pass and corrective second run rather than clean first-pass success.

bounded_claims.claim_text: The reported gains came with materially higher token usage and remained vulnerable to retrieval failures that could mislead planning.
bounded_claims.bounds: The evidence supports a cost and robustness bound, not a positive mechanism of improvement; any reliability advantage is conditional on paying substantially higher token cost and maintaining retrieval quality, since irrelevant retrieval can degrade planning.
